key_insight: In lung CT benchmarks, the choice of segmentation and classifier mattered more than the feature extractor overall, with radiomics and foundation models showing task-specific strengths rather than a universal winner.
clinical_relevance: For practical tumor phenotyping, a two-stage pipeline with Curia plus tumor segmentation and a CatBoost head was the safest default, but the best setup still depended on the specific prediction task.
method: Benchmarked 5 feature extractors: Curia, Curia-2, DINOv3, Radiomics2D, and Radiomics3D.
Compared 7 classification heads: TabPFN, TabICL, XGBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, logistic regression, and Ridge.
Tested 3 segmentation regimes across 5 tasks, using worst-case cross-cohort performance as the primary metric.
evidence_signal: Models were trained on LUNG1 (n=338) and evaluated on an internal test set (n=84) plus external LUNG2 (n=211), focusing on cross-cohort robustness.
watch_item: The recommended default is not universally optimal, and results may shift when tumor delineations are unavailable or when labels are partly derived from the segmentation itself.
paper_title: Foundation Models vs. Radiomics for Lung Computed Tomography: A Benchmark of Feature Extractors, Classification Heads, and Segmentation Choices
